text: This is a step-by-step guide on how to perform a total laparoscopic hysterectomy using just bipolar and scissors. It teaches ambidexterity. It is the gold standard of a laparoscopic hysterectomy. It is the epitome of efficiency and the most cost-effective method. We have divided it into ten steps to follow to achieve the perfect TLH.

The patient is positioned in modified lithotomy, and a uterine manipulator is used to maneuver the uterus. The manipulator pushes the uterus upward, stretching the round ligament perpendicular to the bipolar forceps inserted through the surgeon's left lower port. Coagulation is visually confirmed as the tissue turns white and bubbles decrease in size.

The surgeon uses Metzenbaum scissors in the right hand to cut the coagulated round ligament. The assistant holds the proximal end of the ligament, pulling it downward to expose the anterior leaf of the broad ligament. An anterior U-cut is made using bipolar forceps and scissors to maintain a bloodless field. The assistant lifts the bladder and peritoneum with a grasper, while the vaginal assistant maneuvers the uterus for optimal presentation. The same procedure is repeated on the opposite side, with the energy device handed to the co-surgeon, while the scissors remain in the surgeon's right hand.

The assistant stretches the ovary to expose the fallopian tube and utero-ovarian ligament, which are coagulated and sectioned by the surgeon. On the contralateral side, a small incision is made in the posterior broad ligament, extending parallel to the uterus, positioning the ureter on the lateral border of the created window. The surgeon approaches the right tubo-ovarian structures through the ports, directing towards the window for safe dissection.

With the uterus retroverted and pushed kephalad, the assistant uses a Johan grasper to lift the bladder and peritoneum towards the anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon employs bipolar forceps to dissect underneath until the clear white cervical vesicle tissue is visible, followed by scissors to perform bladder dissection. Fibrosis and scar tissue from a previous cesarean section are observed, prompting dissection from the lateral margin to achieve an avascular plane by removing the D-lease fascia containing small capillaries and veins.

The uterine manipulator is used to stretch and position the uterus, distancing the uterine artery from the ureter. The uterine pedicle is presented perpendicular to the bipolar forceps, which coagulate the area where the utero-sacral ligaments insert. The coagulated pedicle is carefully cut with cold scissors, ensuring minimal cutting to avoid deeper veins that may cause bleeding. The scissors are inverted to separate the pedicle from the cervix. On the right side, the co-surgeon uses bipolar forceps to coagulate the uterine pedicle at the isthmic region. The uterus is anteverted to expose the venous bundle, with bipolar forceps ready to address any bleeding.

The uterus changes color to deep purple or white, indicating readiness for colpotomy. A cup is introduced over the manipulator to highlight the fornices of the vagina. The surgeon uses a monopolar hook with a pure cutting current to perform the anterior colpotomy, while the vaginal assistant maneuvers the uterus to present the fornices. The uterus is slightly anteverted for better access to the lower lateral and posterior margins. The co-surgeon begins slicing through the vaginal layers for colpotomy, ensuring preservation of the pericevical ring above the utero-sacral insertion.

After extracting the uterus through the vagina, the surgeon addresses the adnexal structures. On the left side, a salpingectomy is performed, carefully coagulating and sectioning the tube while preserving ovarian supply. On the right side, an adnexectomy is carried out, with the assistant holding the ovary to stretch the infundibulopelvic ligament. The surgeon ensures the ureter is visualized but not dissected, maintaining a safe distance during the procedure.

The surgeon performs vault closure using an ipsilateral suturing technique, starting from the left angle. A figure-of-eight suture is placed through the head of the utero-sacral ligament for added support. A reasonably sized needle is used to achieve full thickness closure of the vaginal vault. After securing the left angle, the closure continues from the right side, with the needle following its natural curve through the tissue.

The surgeon begins a running continuous closure on the left side of the vaginal vault, ensuring full-thickness closure of the tissue. A toothed needle holder is used to grasp the mucosa effectively, while avoiding locking the suture in areas with visible ooze.

The surgeon introduces a suction cannula to perform a final lavage, removing any residual blood from the surgical field. All vital structures are traced and inspected to ensure no complications before concluding the hysterectomy procedure.
